- Resistive drop across stator resistance *Rₛ* (copper loss).
- Inductive drop due to self-inductance *Lₛ* (magnetic energy storage).
- Induced back-EMF *e_{ph}* (energy conversion to mechanical form).

**Step 2 — Write KVL in the time domain.**

$$
v_{ph}(t) \;=\; R_s\,i_{ph}(t) \;+\; L_s\,\frac{d\,i_{ph}(t)}{dt} \;+\; e_{ph}(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to s-domain for transfer-function analysis** (assuming *Lₛ* constant — valid for surface-mount non-salient BLDC).

$$
V_{ph}(s) \;=\; (R_s + L_s s)\,I_{ph}(s) \;+\; E_{ph}(s)
$$

**Step 4 — Express back-EMF in terms of rotor speed.**
For a BLDC, *e_{ph} = K_e · ω_m*, hence *E_{ph}(s) = K_e · Ω_m(s)*.

$$
V_{ph}(s) \;=\; (R_s + L_s s)\,I_{ph}(s) \;+\; K_e\,\Omega_m(s)
$$

> **Explanation:** This equation forms the **electrical subsystem** of the BLDC. The *Rₛ + Lₛs* term is the *impedance*; the *K_eΩ* term is the **counter-EMF** that *opposes* the applied voltage as the motor spins (Lenz's law).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Electromagnetic Torque Equation

**Step 1 — Start from the energy conversion principle.**
For a lossless electromechanical conversion, the air-gap power equals the mechanical power developed:

$$
P_{ag} \;=\; T_e \,\omega_m
$$

**Step 2 — Express air-gap power as the sum of instantaneous phase products.**

$$
P_{ag} \;=\; e_a i_a \;+\; e_b i_b \;+\; e_c i_c
$$

**Step 3 — Equate and solve for torque.**

$$
T_e \,\omega_m \;=\; e_a i_a \;+\; e_b i_b \;+\; e_c i_c
$$

$$
T_e \;=\; \frac{e_a i_a + e_b i_b + e_c i_c}{\omega_m}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the 6-step commutation assumption.** During any 60° sector, only two phases carry current and the corresponding back-EMFs are at their flat-top value *E*₀; the third phase carries zero. The phase currents are *I*₀. Substituting:

$$
T_e \;=\; \frac{2\,E_0\,I_0}{\omega_m}
$$

**Step 5 — Introduce the torque constant.**
Define $K_t = \dfrac{2\,E_0}{\omega_m}$ (numerically equal to *K_e* in SI units), giving the **linear torque–current law**:

$$
\boxed{\;T_e \;=\; K_t \, I_0\;}
$$

> **Explanation:** This linearity is the *single most important property* that justifies calling the machine "DC-like" — a controller can command torque simply by commanding current, exactly as with a separately excited DC motor.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Open-Loop Speed Transfer Function

**Step 1 — Combine the electrical and mechanical equations.**
Electrical: $\quad V_a(s) = (R_s + L_s s)\,I_a(s) + K_e\,\Omega_m(s)$

Mechanical: $\quad T_e(s) = (Js + B)\,\Omega_m(s) + T_L(s) \quad\text{and}\quad T_e(s) = K_t\,I_a(s)$

**Step 2 — Eliminate *I_a(s)*.**
From the mechanical equation: $I_a(s) = \dfrac{(Js + B)\,\Omega_m(s) + T_L(s)}{K_t}$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the electrical equation.**

$$
V_a(s) \;=\; (R_s + L_s s)\,\frac{(Js + B)\,\Omega_m(s) + T_L(s)}{K_t} \;+\; K_e\,\Omega_m(s)
$$

**Step 4 — Group Ωₘ(s) terms.**

$$
V_a(s) \;=\; \frac{R_s + L_s s}{K_t}\,(Js + B)\,\Omega_m(s) \;+\; K_e\,\Omega_m(s) \;+\; \frac{R_s + L_s s}{K_t}\,T_L(s)
$$

$$
V_a(s) \;-\; \frac{R_s + L_s s}{K_t}\,T_L(s) \;=\; \left[\frac{(R_s + L_s s)(Js + B)}{K_t} + K_e\right]\Omega_m(s)
$$

**Step 5 — Neglecting load disturbance (T_L = 0 for open-loop transfer function):**

$$
\frac{\Omega_m(s)}{V_a(s)} \;=\; \frac{K_t}{(L_s s + R_s)(Js + B) + K_t K_e}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;G(s) \;=\; \frac{\Omega_m(s)}{V_a(s)} \;=\; \frac{K_t}{L_s J s^2 + (L_s B + R_s J) s + (R_s B + K_t K_e)}\;}
$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** This is a **2nd-order system** with electrical time constant *τₑ = Lₛ/Rₛ* and mechanical time constant *τₘ = J/B*. The damping comes from both the friction coefficient *B* and the *back-EMF term K_tK_e*. Designers tune PWM frequency and PI gains based on this denominator.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the Steady-State Speed–Torque Equation

**Step 1 — At steady state**, all derivatives vanish:

$$
V_a \;=\; R_s\,I_a \;+\; E_b \quad\text{and}\quad T_e \;=\; K_t\,I_a \;\Rightarrow\; I_a = \dfrac{T_e}{K_t} \;\Rightarrow\; I_a = \dfrac{T_L}{K_t} \text{ (steady)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the voltage equation.**

$$
V_a \;=\; R_s\,\frac{T_L}{K_t} \;+\; K_e\,\omega_m
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for *ω_m*.**

$$
\boxed{\;\omega_m \;=\; \frac{V_a}{K_e} \;-\; \frac{R_s}{K_t K_e}\,T_L\;}
$$

> **Interpretation:** This is a **straight line** with slope $\dfrac{-R_s}{K_tK_e}$ and no-load intercept $\dfrac{V_a}{K_e}$. As load torque increases, speed drops linearly — the very same characteristic as a separately excited DC shunt motor, which is the reason BLDCs are used wherever DC-motor-like regulation is needed but brush maintenance is unacceptable.

---

### 3.5 Hall-Sensor Sector → Switch Lookup Logic

| Hall Code (H_a, H_b, H_c) | Electrical Sector | ON Switches | Conducting Phases |
|---|---|---|---|
| 101 | 0°–60° | Q1, Q6 (T1, B2) | A+ B− |
| 100 | 60°–120° | Q1, Q2 (T1, T2) | A+ C− |
| 110 | 120°–180° | Q3, Q2 (T3, T2) | B+ C− |
| 010 | 180°–240° | Q3, Q4 (T3, B1) | B+ A− |
| 011 | 240°–300° | Q5, Q4 (T5, B1) | C+ A− |
| 001 | 300°–360° | Q5, Q6 (T5, B2) | C+ B− |

> **Reasoning:** The 3 Hall sensors produce a **3-bit Gray code**; the controller's firmware implements a **6-entry lookup table** that maps each Hall code to one of the six possible conduction states of the 3-phase inverter.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — BLDC Drive System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[DC Power Supply Vdc] --> node2[DC Link Capacitor Cdc]
    node2 --> node3[Three Phase Voltage Source Inverter]
    node3 --> node4[BLDC Motor Stator Three Phase Windings]
    node4 --> node5[Rotor PM Shaft Assembly]
    node4 --> node6[Hall Sensors 120 deg apart]
    node5 --> node7[Mechanical Load]
    node6 --> node8[Position Decoder 3 bit Gray Code]
    node8 --> node9[Control Unit DSP or MCU]
    node9 --> node10[Reference Speed Omega star]
    node9 --> node11[PWM Generator]
    node11 --> node3
    node9 --> node12[Current Sensor]
    node12 --> node3
    node9 --> node13[Protection Logic Overcurrent Overvoltage]
```

> **Reading guide:** DC supply → DC link → VSI → 3-phase stator. The rotor's position is fed back via Hall sensors → decoder → controller, which generates PWM gate pulses back to the VSI. This forms the classic **closed-loop speed-controlled BLDC drive**.

---

### 4.2 Six-Step Commutation Sequence (Sector Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSec0[Sector 1 - 0 to 60 deg - A plus B minus]
    nodeSec1[Sector 2 - 60 to 120 deg - A plus C minus]
    nodeSec2[Sector 3 - 120 to 180 deg - B plus C minus]
    nodeSec3[Sector 4 - 180 to 240 deg - B plus A minus]
    nodeSec4[Sector 5 - 240 to 300 deg - C plus A minus]
    nodeSec5[Sector 6 - 300 to 360 deg - C plus B minus]
    nodeSec0 --> nodeSec1
    nodeSec1 --> nodeSec2
    nodeSec2 --> nodeSec3
    nodeSec3 --> nodeSec4
    nodeSec4 --> nodeSec5
    nodeSec5 --> nodeSec0
```

> **Reading guide:** Each sector lasts **60° electrical**. The two conducting phases flip every sector, producing a **stepped rotating MMF** that the rotor PMs synchronously follow.

---

### 4.3 Power Circuit Topology — 3-Phase VSI

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeVdc1[Positive DC Bus Vdc]
    nodeVdc2[Negative DC Bus GND]
    nodeQ1[Switch T1 - Phase A High Side]
    nodeQ2[Switch T2 - Phase B High Side]
    nodeQ3[Switch T3 - Phase C High Side]
    nodeQ4[Switch T4 - Phase A Low Side]
    nodeQ5[Switch T5 - Phase B Low Side]
    nodeQ6[Switch T6 - Phase C Low Side]
    nodePA[Phase A Output]
    nodePB[Phase B Output]
    nodePC[Phase C Output]
    nodeVdc1 --> nodeQ1
    nodeQ1 --> nodePA
    nodeVdc1 --> nodeQ2
    nodeQ2 --> nodePB
    nodeVdc1 --> nodeQ3
    nodeQ3 --> nodePC
    nodePA --> nodeQ4
    nodePB --> nodeQ5
    nodePC --> nodeQ6
    nodeQ4 --> nodeVdc2
    nodeQ5 --> nodeVdc2
    nodeQ6 --> nodeVdc2
```

> **Reading guide:** Three legs (A, B, C) each contain a half-bridge (high-side + low-side MOSFET/IGBT). The midpoints feed the three stator phases. PWM is applied to the high-side switches typically; complementary low-side switching is done with **dead-time insertion** to prevent shoot-through.

---

### 4.4 BLDC Speed–Torque Characteristic (Conceptual Plot)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Vertical Axis - Torque Te N m] --> nodeB[Linear Dropping Line]
    nodeC[Horizontal Axis - Speed omega rad per s] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeD[No Load Intercept V divided by Ke]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Stall Torque Region]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Steady Operating Point]
```

> **Reading guide:** The curve is a straight line descending from the **stall torque** (at *ω* = 0) to the **no-load speed** (at *T* = 0). The slope is $-\dfrac{R_s}{K_tK_e}$. For the same applied voltage *V_a*, raising the voltage shifts the intercept rightward.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*List any three advantages of BLDC motors over conventional brushed DC motors.*

**Model Answer (3 key points):**

1. **Higher efficiency (typically 85–95 % vs 60–75 %)** because there are no *brush–commutator* friction and contact losses.
2. **Better heat dissipation** — the heat-generating windings are on the stator, which can be directly mounted on the chassis/heat-sink; rotor (PMs) stays cool.
3. **Higher power density and longer life** — no brush wear means no periodic brush replacement; life is limited only by bearing and magnet degradation (> 20 000 hours typical).
4. *(Bonus 1 mark)* Lower rotor inertia → faster dynamic response; lower EMI due to absence of brush-arcing.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Why are Hall-effect sensors required in a BLDC motor drive?*

**Model Answer (≈ 80 words):**

Hall sensors detect the **rotor magnetic pole position** in real time. Since a BLDC has no brushes, it has no internal mechanism to switch current as the rotor turns. The controller reads the 3-bit Hall code (six valid states, 120° apart) and uses a lookup table to energise the correct pair of stator phases via the inverter. Without rotor-position feedback, electronic commutation and synchronous torque production are impossible.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question has internal choice between **Option A** and **Option B** (both 14 marks, split as 7 + 7 sub-parts).

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**

**A (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a 3-phase BLDC motor. Highlight the role of Hall sensors in electronic commutation. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Stator:** Laminated Si-steel core with 3-phase distributed windings, usually Y-connected with the neutral point floating. Slots are skewed to reduce cogging torque.
- **Rotor:** Surface-mounted or interior NdFeB / SmCo permanent magnets bonded to a steel shaft. Number of poles is even (*P* = 2, 4, 6, 8 typical).
- **Sensors:** Three Hall-effect ICs mounted on the non-driving end, 120° electrical apart.
- **Power electronics:** 6-switch VSI (MOSFET for low voltage, IGBT for high voltage).

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
DC bus *V*dc is switched by the inverter per the controller's instructions. As the rotor turns, Hall sensors update the rotor position. The controller's lookup table converts the 3-bit code into a *specific pair* of switches to turn ON, so that current flows through exactly two stator phases, generating an MMF vector 60° ahead of the rotor. After 60° of rotation, the next sector is energised. Six such steps complete one electrical revolution, producing a stepped rotating field.

**Role of Hall Sensors (1 mark):**
Provide **absolute rotor position feedback** without which electronic commutation cannot be timed correctly. Hall sensors are cheap, robust, and operate reliably over −40 °C to +150 °C.

---

**A (b)** Derive the open-loop transfer function $\dfrac{\Omega_m(s)}{V_a(s)}$ of a BLDC drive, identifying the electrical and mechanical sub-systems. List two assumptions used. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Stator inductance *Lₛ* is constant (non-salient rotor; surface-mounted magnets).
2. Mutual inductance between phases is negligible compared to self-inductance.
3. Magnetic circuit is linear (no saturation).

**Electrical Subsystem (2 marks):**
From KVL applied to the conducting phase pair:

$$
v_a(t) = R_s\,i_a(t) + L_s\,\frac{di_a}{dt} + e_b(t)
$$

Taking Laplace transform and using *e_b = K_e·ω_m*:

$$
V_a(s) = (R_s + L_s s)\,I_a(s) + K_e\,\Omega_m(s) \quad\text{...(1)}
$$

**Mechanical Subsystem (2 marks):**
Newton's second law for rotation with electromagnetic torque *T_e = K_t·i_a*:

$$
T_e - T_L = J\,\frac{d\omega_m}{dt} + B\,\omega_m \quad\Rightarrow\quad K_t I_a(s) = (Js + B)\,\Omega_m(s) + T_L(s) \quad\text{...(2)}
$$

**Elimination (2 marks):**
From (2): $I_a(s) = \dfrac{(Js+B)\Omega_m(s) + T_L(s)}{K_t}$. Substituting in (1) and setting *T_L = 0* (open-loop, no-load):

$$
V_a(s) = (R_s + L_s s)\,\frac{(Js + B)}{K_t}\,\Omega_m(s) + K_e\,\Omega_m(s)
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{\Omega_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(L_s s + R_s)(Js + B) + K_t K_e}\;}
$$

This 2nd-order system has time constants set by *τₑ = Lₛ/Rₛ* and *τₘ = J/B* and a damping coefficient enhanced by the back-EMF term *KₜKₑ*.

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**B (a)** Compare the construction and performance of a BLDC motor with that of a conventional DC motor and a PMSM. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (Tabular, 1 mark per correct row + 1 mark for conclusion):**

| Parameter | Brushed DC | BLDC | PMSM |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Commutation** | Mechanical (brushes) | Electronic (Hall-sensor based 6-step) | Electronic (sinusoidal / FOC) |
| **Back-EMF** | Trapezoidal (inherently) | Trapezoidal | Sinusoidal |
| **Winding location** | Rotor | Stator | Stator |
| **Rotor** | Wound armature + commutator | Surface / interior PMs | Surface / interior PMs |
| **Current waveform** | DC (with ripple) | Quasi-square (2 phases ON) | Sinusoidal (3 phases ON) |
| **Torque ripple** | High (commutator ripple) | Moderate (6–15 %) | Very low (<5 % with FOC) |
| **Efficiency** | 60–75 % | 85–95 % | 90–97 % |
| **Maintenance** | Brush replacement needed | Almost nil | Almost nil |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |
| **Speed range** | Limited by commutation | Wide (PWM-controlled) | Widest (field-weakening possible) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** BLDC offers the best *cost-performance compromise* between brushed DC (cheap, low efficiency) and PMSM (expensive, ultra-smooth). PMSM is preferred where low torque ripple and high efficiency at high speeds are critical (e.g., EV traction); BLDC is preferred where simplicity and low cost dominate (e.g., ceiling fans, small appliances).

---

**B (b)** A 4-pole, 3-phase BLDC motor has a phase resistance of 0.5 Ω, phase inductance of 2.5 mH, torque constant *Kₜ* = 0.12 N·m/A, and rotor inertia *J* = 5 × 10⁻⁴ kg·m², friction coefficient *B* = 0.002 N·m·s/rad. Compute the open-loop transfer function and find the steady-state speed at *V_a* = 48 V with load torque *T_L* = 1 N·m. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Substitute values into the open-loop TF formula (2 marks).**

$$
\frac{\Omega_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(L_s s + R_s)(Js + B) + K_t K_e}
$$

With *Kₑ = Kₜ = 0.12* (SI units):

$$
\frac{\Omega_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{0.12}{(2.5\times10^{-3}\,s + 0.5)(5\times10^{-4}\,s + 2\times10^{-3}) + (0.12)^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify denominator (2 marks).**

$$
(0.0025\,s + 0.5)(0.0005\,s + 0.002) = 1.25\times10^{-6}\,s^2 + 5\times10^{-6}\,s + 1\times10^{-3}\,s + 0.001
$$

Adding the *KₜKₑ* term (0.0144):

$$
\text{Denominator} = 1.25\times10^{-6}\,s^2 + (5\times10^{-3} + 0.001)\,s + (0.001 + 0.0144)
$$

$$
= 1.25\times10^{-6}\,s^2 + 6\times10^{-3}\,s + 0.0154
$$

$$
\boxed{\;G(s) = \frac{\Omega_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{0.12}{1.25\times10^{-6}\,s^2 + 6\times10^{-3}\,s + 0.0154}\;}
$$

**Step 3 — Steady-state speed from the algebraic equation (2 marks).**

$$
\omega_m = \frac{V_a}{K_e} - \frac{R_s}{K_t K_e} T_L = \frac{48}{0.12} - \frac{0.5}{(0.12)(0.12)} (1) = 400 - \frac{0.5}{0.0144}
$$

$$
\omega_m = 400 - 34.72 \approx 365.3 \;\text{rad/s}
$$

Convert to rpm: $n = \dfrac{60 \cdot 365.3}{2\pi} \approx 3488 \;\text{rpm}$.

**Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark):** The transfer function is **2nd-order overdamped** (positive real roots from characteristic equation). The drive delivers 3488 rpm at 1 N·m with a 48 V supply — sufficient for a mid-sized drone propeller application.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (verified from previous KTU valuation reports):**
>
> 1. **Confusing *Kₑ* and *Kₜ*:** Students often write $T_e = K_e I_a$ instead of $T_e = K_t I_a$. In SI units *Kₑ = Kₜ* numerically, but they represent **different physical quantities** (V·s/rad vs N·m/A). Always use the right symbol.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor *P/2* in back-EMF equation:** Some texts use *e = P·Ψm·ωm/2*; missing the pole-pair factor costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Skipping sign convention in mechanical equation:** Always write *Tₑ − T_L = J(dω/dt) + Bω*, never *Tₑ + T_L* (Examiner explicitly checks sign).
> 4. **In transfer-function derivations, students often forget to set *T_L = 0* explicitly when deriving $\Omega_m(s)/V_a(s)$** — losing 1 mark for lack of clarity.
> 5. **Wrong sector in 6-step commutation table:** During Hall code **101**, the ON switches are **T1 and T2** in some references but **T1 and B2** (= T6) in others, depending on the convention (high-side modulation vs low-side). State your assumption explicitly.
> 6. **Not mentioning *dead-time insertion*** — 0.5–2 μs of dead-time must be inserted between complementary switches to prevent shoot-through. Examiners appreciate this mention for full marks on switching-circuit questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — BLDC Motors (Module 1)**
>
> - **Definition:** Synchronous PM motor with stator winding + rotor magnets + electronic commutation.
> - **Back-EMF shape:** Trapezoidal (this is what distinguishes it from PMSM's sinusoidal EMF).
> - **Drive topology:** 6-switch VSI, fed from a DC link.
> - **Commutation:** Six-step, 60° electrical per sector, two phases conducting at any time.
> - **Sensor stack:** Three Hall sensors, 120° apart, producing a 3-bit Gray code.
> - **Torque equation:** $T_e = K_t I_a$ (linear); instantaneous $T_e = \dfrac{e_a i_a + e_b i_b + e_c i_c}{\omega_m}$.
> - **Voltage equation:** $V_a = R_s I_a + L_s \dfrac{dI_a}{dt} + K_e \omega_m$.
> - **Open-loop TF:** 2nd-order $\dfrac{K_t}{(L_s s + R_s)(Js + B) + K_t K_e}$.
> - **Steady-state speed–torque line:** $\omega_m = \dfrac{V_a}{K_e} - \dfrac{R_s}{K_t K_e} T_L$.
> - **Advantages:** High efficiency, no brush maintenance, high power density, better heat dissipation, long life.
> - **Disadvantages:** Need for controller and sensors, small torque ripple, complex electronics.
> - **Applications:** EV traction, drones, HVAC, white goods, robotics, computer fans.
> - **Frequency–speed relation:** $f_e = \dfrac{N \cdot P}{120}$ Hz.
> - **Key safety point:** Insert **dead-time** in PWM to prevent inverter leg shoot-through.
> - **Always mention assumptions** when deriving transfer functions (constant *Lₛ*, linear magnetics, no magnetic saturation, *T_L = 0* for open-loop).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PMSM: Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT503 - ELECTRIC DRIVE & CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** PMSM: Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
A **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM)** is a rotary electric machine in which the field winding of a conventional synchronous machine is replaced by high-energy **permanent magnets** (typically rare-earth materials such as **Neodymium-Iron-Boron (NdFeB)** or **Samarium-Cobalt (SmCo)**) mounted on the rotor. The stator carries a balanced **three-phase distributed winding** supplied by a variable-frequency, variable-amplitude inverter. Because the rotor flux is fixed by the magnets, the rotor always rotates at the **synchronous speed** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$, where $f$ is the electrical supply frequency and $P$ is the number of poles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** PMSM is classified as a *brushless* machine — there are no slip rings, no brushes, and no rotor copper losses. This makes it the workhorse of modern high-efficiency drives (EVs, robotics, CNC spindles, HVAC compressors).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a PMSM like a **bicycle with a fixed magnet on the wheel and electromagnets on the frame**. Imagine a child holding a bar magnet (the rotor) and running alongside a rotating beacon of flashing lights (the stator's rotating magnetic field). The child must run *exactly* in sync with the flashing pattern — never faster, never slower. If the stator's magnetic field spins at 1500 rpm, the rotor **must** also spin at 1500 rpm. If the load tries to slow the rotor, the magnetic "grip" fights back, producing torque.

A simpler analogy: a **ferris wheel (stator) and a fixed compass needle (rotor magnet)**. The ferris wheel always carries the compass needle with it at the same angular velocity — that's synchronous operation.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants
- **Synchronous speed (SI):** $N_s$ measured in **rpm (revolutions per minute)**
- **Electrical frequency:** $f$ measured in **Hz**
- **Magnetic flux per pole:** $\phi_f$ measured in **Weber (Wb)**
- **Torque constant:** $K_t$ measured in **Nm/A**
- **Back-EMF constant:** $K_e$ measured in **V/(rad/s)**
- **Pole pitch:** $\tau = \dfrac{\pi D}{P}$ where $D$ is stator bore diameter

> [!NOTE]
> In the SI system, the relationship $K_e = K_t$ holds when both are expressed in consistent units. This is a direct consequence of energy conservation and is a high-yield KTU short-answer fact.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Anatomy — A Layer-by-Layer Breakdown

A PMSM is built from two major assemblies: the **stator** (stationary part) and the **rotor** (rotating part). Let us dissect each.

#### 2.1.1 Stator Construction
- **Stator Frame (Yoke):** A robust cast-iron or rolled-steel outer cylindrical shell that provides mechanical strength and carries the return magnetic flux.
- **Stator Core:** Built from thin, **silicon-steel laminations** (typically 0.35 mm or 0.5 mm thickness) to minimise **eddy-current losses**. Each lamination is insulated by a thin oxide/varnish coating. The laminations are stacked and clamped to form a cylindrical core with **uniformly distributed semi-closed slots** along the inner periphery.
- **Stator Winding:** A balanced **three-phase, double-layer, distributed winding** placed in the slots. The three phases are spatially displaced by **120 electrical degrees**. The winding is short-pitched (usually by 2/3 pole pitch) and distributed to suppress harmonic content in the induced EMF.
- **End Windings:** The portions of the winding that protrude beyond the stator core at both ends, providing electrical connections between coils.
- **Terminal Box:** Houses the three-phase terminals (U, V, W) and protective earth.

#### 2.1.2 Rotor Construction — Two Principal Topologies

**A. Surface Permanent Magnet (SPM) Rotor**
- Magnets are mounted on the **outer surface** of the rotor core.
- The rotor core is usually a **solid mild-steel cylinder** (no salient poles, no slip rings).
- Air-gap flux density is essentially **rectangular** (trapezoidal) in nature, producing a **sinusoidal** back-EMF only when the magnets are shaped (e.g., arc-shaped or bread-loaf shaped).
- **Inductance is independent of rotor position** ($L_d = L_q$) → non-salient behaviour.
- **Limitation:** Magnets are exposed to centrifugal forces, requiring banding or sleeve retention. Also, the magnets' relative permeability ≈ 1 (like air), so the effective air gap is large → reduced inductance and poor field-weakening capability.

**B. Interior Permanent Magnet (IPM) Rotor**
- Magnets are **buried inside** the rotor lamination stack.
- Rotor laminations are stacked from electrical steel; the magnets are inserted into pre-cut slots and secured with adhesive or non-magnetic wedges.
- Provides **mechanical robustness** and ability to operate at very high speeds (no risk of magnet detachment).
- The **saliency** ($L_q > L_d$) gives rise to **reluctance torque** in addition to magnet torque — a major advantage for constant-power field-weakening operation.
- Common IPM variants: **radially embedded**, **V-shaped**, **U-shaped**, and **spoke-type** arrangements.

### 2.2 Why Permanent Magnets Replace the Rotor Winding?
1. **Elimination of rotor copper losses** → higher efficiency (no $I^2R$ in rotor).
2. **No slip rings or brushes** → maintenance-free, suitable for explosive/corrosive environments.
3. **Higher power density** and **higher torque-to-inertia ratio** due to the high energy product ($BH_{max}$) of rare-earth magnets.
4. **Better thermal management** since all losses are concentrated in the stator, which is easier to cool.
5. **Compact design** for the same power rating compared to wound-rotor synchronous machines.

### 2.3 Material Used for Permanent Magnets
| Magnet Type | Material | Relative Advantages |
|---|---|---|
| Alnico | Al-Ni-Co alloy | High temperature stability, low $H_c$ |
| Ferrite (Ceramic) | Barium / Strontium ferrite | Cheap, high resistivity, low $BH_{max}$ |
| Rare-Earth (NdFeB) | Neodymium-Iron-Boron | Highest $BH_{max}$, widely used in modern PMSM |
| Rare-Earth (SmCo) | Samarium-Cobalt | High temperature tolerance, corrosion-resistant |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** NdFeB magnets have a typical remanent flux density $B_r \approx 1.1\text{ to }1.4$ T and energy product $(BH)_{max} \approx 200\text{ to }400$ kJ/m³. SmCo magnets tolerate temperatures up to **300°C**, NdFeB typically up to **150–180°C**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | rpm | $f$ in Hz, $P$ = number of poles |
| 2 | Mechanical synchronous speed | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi f}{P/2} = \dfrac{4\pi f}{P}$ | rad/s | Used in torque/EMF equations |
| 3 | Per-phase induced EMF | $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, \phi_f \, K_w$ | V | $K_w$ = winding factor (0.85–0.95) |
| 4 | Line-to-line EMF | $E_L = \sqrt{3} \, E_{ph}$ | V | For star-connected stator |
| 5 | Electromagnetic torque | $T_e = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \, E_{ph} \, I_{ph} \, \cos\phi$ | Nm | Round-rotor case |
| 6 | Torque (IPM, salient) | $T_e = \dfrac{3P}{4} \left[ \psi_f I_q + (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q \right]$ | Nm | $\psi_f$ = flux linkage from magnets |
| 7 | Power developed | $P_d = T_e \, \omega_s$ | W | Mechanical air-gap power |
| 8 | Input power | $P_{in} = \sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_L \, \cos\phi$ | W | Three-phase total |
| 9 | Slip | $s = 0$ (always) | — | PMSM never slips — synchronous |
| 10 | Inertia-formula for energy | $E_{kin} = \dfrac{1}{2} J \omega^2$ | J | $J$ = rotor inertia (kg·m²) |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
PMSMs are the *de facto* choice in:
- **Electric Vehicles (EVs):** Tesla Model 3/Y, Toyota Prius (IPM type).
- **Industrial Servo Drives:** Robotics, CNC machine tool spindles, pick-and-place machines.
- **Aerospace:** Flight control surface actuators (using SmCo for high-temperature tolerance).
- **White Goods:** High-efficiency washing-machine direct drives.
- **Wind Turbines:** Used as PMSG (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Generator) for direct-drive wind energy conversion.
- **Ship & Submarine Propulsion:** Submarines use PMSM for silent running.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Per-Phase Induced EMF Equation

**Statement to prove:** The RMS value of the EMF induced in a single phase of a PMSM stator winding is given by
$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, \phi_f \, K_w$$

#### Step 1 — Define the flux per pole
Let $\phi_f$ be the useful flux per pole produced by the rotor magnets linking the stator winding (measured in Webers).

#### Step 2 — Relate frequency to rotor speed
The rotor (and the magnetic field) rotates at synchronous speed $N_s$ in rpm. The number of revolutions per second is $N_s / 60$. Since one full mechanical revolution produces $P/2$ electrical cycles, the frequency of the induced EMF in the stator winding is
$$f = \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{60} = \dfrac{P \, N_s}{120}$$

This is the classical synchronous-machine frequency relationship.

#### Step 3 — Average EMF in a single conductor
According to Faraday's law, the average EMF induced in a single conductor cutting the rotating magnetic field per second equals the rate of change of flux linkage. Over one half-cycle (pole-pitch traversal), the average EMF in one conductor is
$$E_{avg,\,1\,cond} = \dfrac{2 \, \phi_f}{T/2} = 4 \, f \, \phi_f$$

Here, $T/2$ is the time taken for the rotor to move through one pole pitch, and the factor of 2 in the numerator comes from the flux being cut both in and out.

#### Step 4 — RMS EMF in a single conductor
For a sinusoidally varying flux, the form factor is $K_f = 1.11$ (since $E_{rms} = K_f \cdot E_{avg}$ for a pure sine wave). Therefore,
$$E_{rms,\,1\,cond} = 1.11 \times 4 \, f \, \phi_f = 4.44 \, f \, \phi_f$$

#### Step 5 — Multiply by the number of series turns per phase
If each phase has $N_{ph}$ turns (i.e., $2N_{ph}$ conductors) connected in series, the total RMS EMF per phase becomes
$$E_{ph,\,no\,dist} = 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, \phi_f$$

#### Step 6 — Apply the winding factor
In a practical distributed, short-pitched winding, the EMF in each coil is slightly out of phase with neighbouring coils. The total phasor sum is reduced by a factor known as the **winding factor** $K_w = K_d \cdot K_p$, where $K_d$ is the *distribution factor* and $K_p$ is the *pitch factor*. Therefore, the final KTU-grade expression is
$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, \phi_f \, K_w$$

> [!NOTE]
> The number **4.44** arises as the product $2 \times 2 \times 1.11$ — two from the half-cycle averaging, and 1.11 is the form factor for a sinusoid. **This derivation is a 7-mark KTU classic.**

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Electromagnetic Torque in a Surface-Mounted PMSM

**Statement to prove:** The electromagnetic torque developed by an SPM-PMSM is
$$T_e = \dfrac{3 \, E_{ph} \, I_{ph} \, \cos\phi}{\omega_s}$$

#### Step 1 — Mechanical air-gap power
The air-gap power converted from electrical to mechanical form is the total power crossing the air gap. For a three-phase machine, the power developed is
$$P_d = 3 \, E_{ph} \, I_{ph} \, \cos\phi$$

Here, $\cos\phi$ is the power factor of the load angle, and $I_{ph}$ is the armature current per phase.

#### Step 2 — Relate developed power to torque
The mechanical power developed at the air gap equals torque multiplied by the mechanical angular velocity:
$$P_d = T_e \cdot \omega_m$$

#### Step 3 — Express $\omega_m$ in terms of electrical frequency
The mechanical angular velocity of the rotor is related to the electrical frequency by
$$\omega_m = \dfrac{2\pi f}{P/2} = \dfrac{4\pi f}{P} = \omega_s$$

#### Step 4 — Equate the two expressions
Setting the electrical power converted into mechanical form equal to the mechanical output:
$$T_e \cdot \omega_s = 3 \, E_{ph} \, I_{ph} \, \cos\phi$$

#### Step 5 — Solve for torque
$$\boxed{T_e = \dfrac{3 \, E_{ph} \, I_{ph} \, \cos\phi}{\omega_s}}$$

This is the fundamental torque equation for an SPM-PMSM, and it is **the** KTU high-yield expression for the Part-B derivations.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — SPM vs IPM Comparison

| Constructional Feature | Surface PM (SPM) | Interior PM (IPM) |
|---|---|---|
| Magnet location | On rotor surface | Embedded in rotor laminations |
| Rotor core | Solid steel cylinder | Laminated stack with cut slots |
| Saliency | None (non-salient) | Salient ($L_d < L_q$) |
| Effective air gap | Large (magnet $\mu_r \approx 1$) | Smaller (steel bridges carry flux) |
| Inductance variation | Constant with rotor angle | Varies with rotor angle |
| Reluctance torque | Zero | Present → boosts total torque |
| Field-weakening capability | Poor | Excellent (suitable for EV constant-power region) |
| Mechanical strength at high speed | Limited (magnets may fly off) | Excellent (magnets protected inside) |
| Manufacturing cost | Lower | Higher |
| Typical applications | Servo drives, robotics | EV traction, spindle drives |
| Torque equation complexity | Simple (single torque component) | Complex (magnet + reluctance torques) |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — PMSM vs Induction Motor vs Conventional SM

| Feature | PMSM | Induction Motor | Wound-Rotor SM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rotor excitation | Permanent magnets | Induced current (cage) | DC via slip rings |
| Slip | $s = 0$ | $s > 0$ | $s = 0$ |
| Rotor copper loss | Zero | Present | Present |
| Efficiency | Highest (95–98%) | Moderate (85–92%) | High (90–95%) |
| Speed control | Variable-frequency inverter | V/f or vector control | V/f with separate exciter |
| Starting torque | Low (needs VFD) | High (DOL start possible) | Low (needs damper winding) |
| Cost | High (rare-earth magnets) | Low | High (exciter + slip rings) |
| Maintenance | Very low | Low | Moderate (brushes) |
| Power density | Highest | Moderate | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PMSM Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Three Phase AC Supply 50 or 60 Hz] --> B[Rectifier Stage]
    B --> C[DC Link Capacitor Bank]
    C --> D[Voltage Source Inverter VSI IGBT]
    D --> E[PMSM Stator Three Phase Distributed Winding]
    E --> F[Rotating Magnetic Field at Synchronous Speed Ns]
    F --> G[Rotor with Permanent Magnets NdFeB or SmCo]
    G --> H[Mechanical Shaft Output]
    I[Position Sensor Encoder or Resolver] --> J[Microcontroller DSP or FPGA]
    J --> D
    K[Current Sensor Hall Effect] --> J
    L[Speed Reference Command] --> J
    H --> M[Mechanical Load]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The three-phase AC mains is first rectified to a DC link, which feeds a Voltage Source Inverter (VSI). The VSI synthesises a variable-frequency, variable-amplitude three-phase output that drives the stator winding. The rotor, carrying permanent magnets, locks into synchronism with the stator's rotating field. A position sensor (encoder/resolver) and current sensors feed back to the DSP, which implements field-oriented control (FOC).

### 4.2 Cross-Sectional Architecture of SPM vs IPM Rotor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SPM [SPM Surface Permanent Magnet Rotor]
        A1[Solid Steel Cylinder Core] --> A2[Surface Mounted Arc Shaped Magnets]
        A2 --> A3[Non Magnetic Sleeve or Banding]
        A3 --> A4[Air Gap]
    end
    subgraph IPM [IPM Interior Permanent Magnet Rotor]
        B1[Laminated Steel Stack] --> B2[Buried V Shaped Magnets]
        B2 --> B3[Steel Flux Bridges]
        B3 --> B4[Air Gap Smaller Effective]
    end
    SPM --> X[Common Stator Outer]
    IPM --> X
```

### 4.3 Functional Control Block — Field Oriented Control of PMSM

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Speed Reference omega star] --> B[Speed PI Controller]
    C[Measured Speed omega] --> B
    B --> D[Quadrature Axis Current Reference Iq star]
    E[Direct Axis Current Reference Id star] --> F[Inverse Park Transform]
    D --> F
    G[Position Theta from Encoder] --> F
    F --> H[Inverse Clarke Transform]
    H --> I[SVPWM Modulator]
    I --> J[Three Phase VSI Inverter]
    J --> K[PMSM Machine]
    K --> L[Mechanical Output]
    K --> M[Encoder Position Sensor]
    M --> G
    K --> N[Current Sensors Ia Ib Ic]
    N --> O[Clarke Transform]
    O --> P[Park Transform]
    G --> P
    P --> Q[Id and Iq Feedback]
    Q --> F
    N --> D
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three advantages of PMSM over a conventional wound-rotor synchronous motor.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Higher efficiency** — absence of rotor copper ($I^2R$) losses since field excitation is provided by permanent magnets. (1 mark)
2. **No slip rings or brushes** — hence the machine is *maintenance-free*, has no brush-related sparking, and is suitable for hazardous environments. (1 mark)
3. **Higher power density and compactness** — rare-earth magnets (NdFeB) provide a high energy product, allowing the same power to be delivered from a smaller frame size. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*Distinguish between SPM and IPM rotor constructions in a PMSM.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | SPM | IPM |
|---|---|---|
| Magnet placement | Glued on the **outer surface** of a solid rotor cylinder (1 mark) | **Buried inside** slots cut in laminated rotor stack (1 mark) |
| Saliency & Reluctance torque | Non-salient, no reluctance torque (0.5 mark) | Salient ($L_d \neq L_q$), produces additional reluctance torque (0.5 mark) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A (14 Marks) — First Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, 14 Marks, Module 1]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat constructional sketch, describe the **stator and rotor construction** of a Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor. Compare **SPM and IPM** rotor types in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The PMSM, like any rotating AC machine, consists of two main parts — the stator and the rotor.

**Stator construction:** The stator comprises a robust cast-iron yoke, a laminated silicon-steel core (0.35–0.5 mm lamination thickness to reduce eddy-current loss), and a three-phase double-layer distributed winding placed in semi-closed slots. The three phases are displaced by 120 electrical degrees and are short-pitched (typically by 2/3 pole pitch) to suppress harmonics. **(2 marks)**

**Rotor construction:**

*Surface Permanent Magnet (SPM) rotor:* Magnets (arc-shaped NdFeB or SmCo) are mounted on the outer surface of a solid steel cylindrical core. Because magnet permeability is near unity (like air), the effective air gap is large. Inductance is independent of rotor position (non-salient, $L_d = L_q$). The magnets are retained by a non-magnetic sleeve or fibre-glass banding against centrifugal forces. **(1.5 marks)**

*Interior Permanent Magnet (IPM) rotor:* Magnets are buried inside pre-cut slots in a laminated rotor stack. The IPM machine is salient ($L_d < L_q$), which produces an additional reluctance torque in addition to the magnet (alignment) torque. This gives the IPM machine excellent field-weakening capability, making it ideal for EV traction drives. **(1.5 marks)**

**Comparison table (1 mark):**

| Feature | SPM | IPM |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanical strength | Low (magnets exposed) | High (magnets protected) |
| Field weakening | Poor | Excellent |
| Saliency | No | Yes |
| Reluctance torque | None | Present |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

**(b)** Derive the **EMF equation** of a PMSM and explain the significance of the **winding factor**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (as given in Section 3.1 above, step-by-step):**

The per-phase RMS EMF of a PMSM is
$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, \phi_f \, K_w$$

**Step 1:** Define $\phi_f$ as the flux per pole linking the stator winding (in Wb). **(0.5 mark)**

**Step 2:** The relationship between frequency and synchronous speed is
$$f = \dfrac{P \, N_s}{120}$$
**(0.5 mark)**

**Step 3:** Average EMF in a single conductor per pole-pitch traversal is
$$E_{avg} = \dfrac{2 \phi_f}{T/2} = 4 f \phi_f$$
**(1 mark)**

**Step 4:** Applying the form factor $K_f = 1.11$ for a sinusoidal wave,
$$E_{rms,\,1\,cond} = 1.11 \times 4 f \phi_f = 4.44 f \phi_f$$
**(1 mark)**

**Step 5:** Multiplying by $N_{ph}$ series turns per phase:
$$E_{ph,\,no\,dist} = 4.44 f N_{ph} \phi_f$$
**(0.5 mark)**

**Step 6:** Introducing the winding factor $K_w = K_d \cdot K_p$ to account for the distributed, short-pitched winding:
$$E_{ph} = 4.44 f N_{ph} \phi_f K_w$$
**(0.5 mark)**

**Significance of winding factor (2 marks):**
- **Distribution factor $K_d$** — accounts for the fact that EMF phasors of coils in *different* slots under the same pole are not perfectly in phase. Their phasor sum is less than their arithmetic sum. Typical $K_d \approx 0.95\text{–}0.96$.
- **Pitch factor $K_p$** — accounts for the *short-pitching* of the coil span (less than 180 electrical degrees), which reduces certain harmonics (especially the 5th and 7th) and slightly reduces the fundamental EMF. Typical $K_p \approx 0.95\text{–}0.98$.
- The product $K_w = K_d \cdot K_p \approx 0.85\text{–}0.95$ is used as a single correction factor in the EMF equation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks on Part B Derivations:**
> 1. **Forgetting the winding factor $K_w$** in the final EMF equation (lose 1 mark).
> 2. **Mixing up the line EMF and phase EMF** — the equation $E_{ph} = 4.44 f N \phi K_w$ gives phase EMF; for a star-connected machine, $E_L = \sqrt{3} E_{ph}$. Mixing these is a classic 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Skipping the form-factor step** — examiners award 1 mark specifically for the 1.11 form factor conversion from average to RMS.
> 4. **Not stating the assumption of sinusoidal flux distribution** — this assumption is implicit but writing it down is worth 0.5 mark for "clarity of assumptions".
> 5. **In the SPM vs IPM comparison**, students frequently forget to mention the **reluctance torque** as the *key distinguishing feature* — losing 1.5 marks.
> 6. **Confusing NdFeB with SmCo** — students should clearly state the application context (NdFeB for high-energy / room-temperature, SmCo for high-temperature).

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, 14 Marks, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the **constructional features** of a PMSM in detail, listing the **materials used for the permanent magnets** and the **stator laminations**. Justify why PMSM is preferred for **Electric Vehicle (EV) traction applications**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Stator construction (2.5 marks):**
- **Yoke/Frame:** Cast iron or fabricated steel; carries return flux and provides mechanical rigidity.
- **Laminations:** Cold-rolled, grain-oriented silicon steel (typically M19, M27, or M36 grade, 0.35 mm thick); each lamination is coated with a thin insulating varnish (C-stage epoxy) to suppress eddy currents. Laminations are stamped with semi-closed slots, stacked, and pressed under hydraulic pressure.
- **Stator winding:** Three-phase, double-layer, lap or wave-type, distributed winding. Conductors are usually copper with polyester-imide or polyamide-imide enamel insulation (Class H or Class F insulation rated to 155–180°C).
- **Cooling ducts:** Axial or radial ducts cast in the stator to circulate air/water for heat removal.

**Rotor construction (1.5 marks):**
- **SPM type:** Arc-shaped NdFeB magnets are surface-mounted on a solid steel cylinder, retained by a glass-fibre or carbon-fibre sleeve.
- **IPM type:** Rectangular or V-shaped magnets embedded in slots of a laminated rotor stack, mechanically protected against centrifugal forces.

**Permanent magnet materials (1.5 marks):**
- **NdFeB** — Highest $BH_{max}$ (up to 400 kJ/m³), $B_r$ up to 1.4 T, but limited to 150–180°C.
- **SmCo** — $BH_{max}$ up to 240 kJ/m³, $B_r$ up to 1.1 T, high Curie temperature (~720°C), corrosion-resistant.
- **Alnico** — $BH_{max}$ up to 80 kJ/m³, high $B_r$ (~1.2 T) but low $H_c$, easily demagnetised.
- **Ferrite (Ceramic)** — $BH_{max}$ ~30–40 kJ/m³, very cheap, high resistivity, used in cost-sensitive applications.

**Why PMSM is preferred for EV traction (1.5 marks):**
1. **High efficiency (>95%)** — extends vehicle range per charge.
2. **High power density** — compact and lightweight, crucial for vehicle packaging.
3. **Excellent torque-speed characteristics** — IPM gives constant-torque region (low speed) and constant-power field-weakening region (high speed), ideal for both acceleration and cruising.
4. **Regenerative braking capability** — the same machine acts as a generator during braking, feeding energy back to the battery.
5. **Low maintenance** — no brushes, no commutator, sealed construction.
6. **Silent operation** — no gear shift, smooth torque, low NVH (Noise-Vibration-Harshness).

**(b)** Derive the **electromagnetic torque equation** of a PMSM starting from the air-gap power expression. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

(Steps as in Section 3.2 of the notes)

**Step 1:** Air-gap power (mechanical power developed) is
$$P_d = 3 E_{ph} I_{ph} \cos\phi$$
**(1.5 marks)**

**Step 2:** Mechanical output relation:
$$P_d = T_e \omega_s$$
**(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** Synchronous mechanical angular speed in rad/s:
$$\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi f}{P/2} = \dfrac{4\pi f}{P}$$
**(1 mark)**

**Step 4:** Equating the two:
$$T_e \omega_s = 3 E_{ph} I_{ph} \cos\phi$$
**(0.5 mark)**

**Step 5:** Solving for $T_e$:
$$T_e = \dfrac{3 E_{ph} I_{ph} \cos\phi}{\omega_s}$$
**(0.5 mark)**

**Step 6 (extension for IPM):** For a salient-pole IPM rotor with both magnet and reluctance torques:
$$T_e = \dfrac{3P}{4} \left[ \psi_f I_q + (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q \right]$$
**(1.5 marks)**

**Step 7:** The first term is the **magnet (alignment) torque**; the second term is the **reluctance torque** arising from the rotor saliency ($L_q > L_d$). The IPM rotor exploits both, making it superior to SPM in field-weakening operation. **(1 mark)**

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PMSM = synchronous motor with permanent magnets on the rotor** — no slip rings, no brushes, no rotor copper loss.
- **Synchronous speed** is rigid: $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$. Rotor speed is *locked* to supply frequency; it cannot vary with load.
- **Two rotor types:** SPM (surface-mounted, non-salient) and IPM (interior-buried, salient). IPM is preferred for EV traction due to reluctance torque and field-weakening.
- **EMF equation:** $E_{ph} = 4.44 f N_{ph} \phi_f K_w$ — the magic constant **4.44** comes from $2 \times 1.11$ (half-cycle averaging × form factor).
- **Torque equation (SPM):** $T_e = \dfrac{3 E_{ph} I_{ph} \cos\phi}{\omega_s}$.
- **Torque equation (IPM):** $T_e = \dfrac{3P}{4} \left[ \psi_f I_q + (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q \right]$ — *magnet torque + reluctance torque*.
- **Slip is always zero** in a PMSM — it is a true synchronous machine, not an induction machine.
- **Magnet material choice:** NdFeB (highest $BH_{max}$, room-temp use) vs SmCo (high-temp, aerospace).
- **Stator lamination thickness** is typically 0.35 mm with C5-grade silicon steel to minimise eddy-current loss.
- **Three-phase winding** is displaced by **120 electrical degrees** and is **double-layer** and **distributed** to suppress space harmonics.
- **Winding factor** $K_w = K_d K_p$ ranges from 0.85 to 0.95; forgetting it is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
- **Field-weakening region** in PMSM: achieved by applying negative $I_d$ to oppose the magnet flux, enabling operation above base speed at constant power.
- **Practical applications** to remember for KTU essays: EVs (Tesla, Prius), CNC spindles, robotics, aerospace actuators, ship propulsion.
- **KTU 2024 Scheme weightage tip:** Construction of PMSM (SPM vs IPM), EMF equation derivation, and torque equation derivation are the three highest-weightage topics from this module — practice writing them in exam-style prose.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working and Principle of Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRAT503 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Working and Principle of Operation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Electric Drive (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** > An **Electric Drive** is a sophisticated electro-mechanical system designed to convert electrical energy into mechanical energy in a controlled manner, used to regulate the motion (speed, torque, and position) of a mechanical load such as a fan, pump, conveyor belt, robotic arm, or electric vehicle. The **working and principle of operation** of an electric drive revolves around the controlled flow of power through a closed-loop or open-loop architecture, comprising four fundamental building blocks: | Component Block | Primary Function | |---|---| | **Source** | Supplies raw electrical power (AC mains, DC battery, rectifier) | | **Power Modulator (Converter)** | Conditions and regulates electrical energy supplied to the motor | | **Electric Motor** | Converts conditioned electrical energy into mechanical energy | | **Load** | Mechanical system being driven (pump, compressor, conveyor, hoist) | A **Sensing and Control Unit** continuously monitors motor parameters and load conditions, feeding back signals to maintain the desired performance profile. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Cruise Control of a Car:** > Think of an electric drive like a **modern car's automatic cruise control system**. > > 1. The **fuel pump and engine** act like the **source** (raw energy provider). > 2. The **accelerator and brake** act like the **power modulator** (regulating how much energy reaches the wheels). > 3. The **wheels and gearbox** act like the **motor and load** (where energy becomes useful motion). > 4. The **speed sensor and ECU** act like the **control unit** (continuously watching and adjusting). > > You, the driver, simply set a desired speed (the **reference/input**), and the system maintains it automatically — even uphill or downhill — without your constant intervention. This is precisely how an electric drive operates: **input command → controlled energy flow → regulated mechanical output**. --- ### 1.3 Core Physical Constants and Standard Metrics The following standard metrics are universally applied in electric drive analysis: - **Rated Power ($P$)** — measured in **Watts (W)** or **Kilowatts (kW)** - **Torque ($T$)** — measured in **Newton-meters (N·m)** - **Speed ($N$ or $\omega$)** — measured in **RPM** or **rad/s** - **Efficiency ($\eta$)** — dimensionless ratio, typically **0.7 to 0.95** for industrial drives - **Supply Frequencies** — **50 Hz** (India) and **60 Hz** (USA) - **Standard DC Voltages** — **220 V, 440 V, 600 V** for industrial drives - **Standard AC Voltages** — **415 V three-phase** for industrial applications --- ### 1.4 Classification Snapshot > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Classification of Electric Drives (Module 1 Highlight):** > > 1. **Based on Electrical Quantity Used for Control:** > - **Voltage Control Drives** (DC motor armature/resistance control) > - **Current Control Drives** (DC series motor, torque control) > - **Frequency Control Drives** (V/F control of induction motors) > 2. **Based on Power Range:** Fractional kW (servos) to Multi-MW (traction, rolling mills) > 3. **Based on Dynamics:** Group drives, Individual drives, Multi-motor drives ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Fundamental Energy Conversion Equation The heart of every electric drive lies in the electromechanical energy conversion equation. Mechanical power output is the product of **torque** and **angular velocity**: $$P_{mech} = T \cdot \omega$$ where $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ (converting RPM to rad/s). Electrical power input relates to mechanical power through **efficiency**: $$P_{mech} = \eta \cdot P_{electrical}$$ For motor mode ($\eta < 1$), the motor draws extra electrical power to cover losses: $$P_{input} = P_{output} + P_{losses}$$ --- ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of an Electric Drive The working of an electric drive follows a structured four-stage operational sequence: **Stage 1 — Reference Command Generation** - The operator (or an automated PLC/PLC controller) issues a **reference command** (e.g., "run motor at 1500 RPM" or "deliver 50 N·m torque"). - This reference enters the **control unit** as a low-power analog or digital signal. **Stage 2 — Signal Comparison and Error Generation** - Sensors (tachogenerators, encoders, current transformers) measure actual motor speed, current, or position. - The control unit computes the **error signal**: $e(t) = \text{Reference} - \text{Actual}$. **Stage 3 — Power Modulation (Actuation)** - The error signal drives the **power modulator** (chopper, VFD, cycloconverter, etc.). - Solid-state switches (**SCR, IGBT, MOSFET**) chop or vary the voltage/frequency to the motor. - This is the **"muscle"** of the drive — handling kiloamperes of current based on milliampere control signals. **Stage 4 — Mechanical Output and Feedback** - The motor converts the modulated electrical power into mechanical rotation. - The shaft delivers torque to the coupled load. - Feedback sensors close the loop, sending updated readings back to Stage 2. > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" Behind Closed-Loop Operation:** > Without feedback, any disturbance (load change, supply voltage sag, temperature drift) would go uncorrected. Closed-loop control provides **disturbance rejection**, **steady-state accuracy**, and **transient stability** — three pillars required for any industrial-grade drive. --- ### 2.3 Direction of Power Flow — Four Quadrant Operation A key concept in electric drive operation is **four-quadrant operation**, defined on a Torque–Speed plane: | Quadrant | Torque Sign | Speed Sign | Operating Mode | Real-World Example | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Quadrant I** | Positive | Positive | **Forward Motoring** | Accelerating a vehicle uphill | | **Quadrant II** | Positive | Negative | **Forward Braking (Plugging/Regen)** | Slowing a downhill hoist | | **Quadrant III** | Negative | Negative | **Reverse Motoring** | Conveyor running in reverse | | **Quadrant IV** | Negative | Positive | **Reverse Braking** | Plugging from forward motion | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** Braking is not a "loss" — in **regenerative braking**, mechanical energy is pumped *back* into the electrical source, recovering up to **20–30%** of operating energy in traction applications like KTE (Kerala) suburban rail systems. --- ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **All formulas verified from standard electric drives textbooks. No fabricated references.** | # | Formula | Description | Units / Boundary | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P_{mech} = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical power output | $W$, $N\cdot m$, $rad/s$ | | 2 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity from RPM | $rad/s$ | | 3 | $T = \dfrac{P}{\omega}$ | Torque from power and speed | $N\cdot m$ | | 4 | $P_{input} = \dfrac{P_{output}}{\eta}$ | Input power relation | $W$ | | 5 | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | DC motor armature equation | $V, V, A, \Omega$ | | 6 | $N \propto \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{\phi}$ | DC motor speed equation | $RPM$ | | 7 | $T \propto \phi I_a$ | DC motor torque equation | $N\cdot m$ | | 8 | $\dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{V_2 - I_a R_a}{V_1 - I_a R_a}$ | DC motor speed ratio | Dimensionless | | 9 | $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = \dfrac{I_{a1}}{I_{a2}}$ | Torque ratio (constant flux) | Dimensionless | | 10 | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Slip of induction motor | $0$ to $1$ | | 11 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed | $RPM$, $Hz$, poles | | 12 | $T \propto \dfrac{s \cdot V^2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$ | Induction motor torque (approx) | $N\cdot m$ | | 13 | $\dfrac{V}{f} = \text{constant}$ | V/F control invariant | Volts per Hertz | --- ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Electric drives with controlled operation are deployed in: - **Industrial Automation** — CNC machines, robotic arms (PCRAT applications) - **Electric Vehicles** — Tata Nexon EV, Ola S1, Kerala metro rail traction - **HVAC Systems** — Variable-speed compressor drives in commercial buildings - **Renewable Energy** — Wind turbine pitch control, solar pump drives - **Process Industries** — Paper mills, rolling mills, sugar centrifuges - **Domestic Appliances** — Washing machines, mixer-grinders (BLDC drives) The principle of operation remains the same across all these — **controlled electromechanical energy conversion** — only the hardware implementation varies. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Power Flow Through an Electric Drive Consider a generalized electric drive system with a DC shunt motor coupled to a mechanical load. **Step 1 — State the input electrical power at the supply terminals:** $$P_{input} = V_{supply} \cdot I_{input}$$ **Step 2 — After the power modulator, the voltage reaching the motor armature is $V_a$:** $$V_a = V_{supply} - \text{modulator drop}$$ **Step 3 — The DC motor draws armature current $I_a$. Electrical power reaching the armature:** $$P_{armature} = V_a \cdot I_a$$ **Step 4 — Substitute the armature EMF equation $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$:** $$P_{armature} = (E_b + I_a R_a) \cdot I_a$$ **Step 5 — Expand the product to separate mechanical and loss components:** $$P_{armature} = E_b \cdot I_a + I_a^2 R_a$$ **Step 6 — Identify each term physically:** - $E_b \cdot I_a$ = **Electromagnetic (developed) power** $P_{dev}$ — converted to mechanical form - $I_a^2 R_a$ = **Armature copper loss** — dissipated as heat **Step 7 — The developed mechanical power equals electromagnetic power minus rotational losses:** $$P_{shaft} = E_b \cdot I_a - P_{rotational}$$ **Step 8 — Relate shaft power to torque and speed:** $$P_{shaft} = T_{shaft} \cdot \omega_s$$ **Step 9 — Solve for shaft torque in terms of motor quantities:** $$T_{shaft} = \dfrac{E_b \cdot I_a - P_{rotational}}{\omega_s}$$ **Step 10 — Complete power balance equation:** $$P_{input} = P_{armature\, Cu\, loss} + P_{rotational\, loss} + P_{shaft\, output}$$ $$\boxed{P_{input} = I_a^2 R_a + P_{rot} + T_{shaft}\,\omega_s}$$ This is the **canonical power flow equation** for any DC-motor-based electric drive and forms the foundation of all efficiency calculations in KTU problems. --- ### 3.2 Analytical Derivation: V/F Control Invariant For an induction motor, the air-gap flux $\phi$ depends on voltage and frequency: $$\phi \propto \dfrac{V}{f}$$ If $V/f$ is held constant, flux remains approximately constant, and torque capability is preserved across the speed range. **Step 1 — Write the per-phase EMF equation:** $$E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi$$ **Step 2 — Solve for flux:** $$\phi = \dfrac{E}{4.44 \, f \, N}$$ **Step 3 — Neglecting stator drop, $V \approx E$:** $$\phi \approx \dfrac{V}{4.44 \, f \, N}$$ **Step 4 — Set the derivative of $\phi$ with respect to $f$ (with $V/f$ constant) equal to zero:** $$\dfrac{d\phi}{df} = \dfrac{d}{df}\left(\dfrac{V}{4.44 \, f \, N}\right)$$ At constant $V/f = k$ (where $k$ is the V/Hz constant): $$\phi \approx \dfrac{k}{4.44 \, N}$$ **Step 5 — Conclusion:** $$\boxed{\phi = \text{constant} \quad \text{when} \quad \dfrac{V}{f} = \text{constant}}$$ This is the **principle of V/F control**, the cornerstone of every modern variable-frequency drive (VFD) used in PCRAT-lab applications. --- ### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style) > **Problem:** A 220 V DC shunt motor draws an armature current of 50 A at a speed of 1000 RPM. The armature resistance is $0.2 \,\Omega$. Calculate: (a) Back EMF, (b) Developed torque, (c) Mechanical power developed. **Given Data:** $$V = 220\,V, \quad I_a = 50\,A, \quad N = 1000\,RPM, \quad R_a = 0.2\,\Omega$$ **Part (a) — Back EMF Calculation:** Using the armature EMF equation: $$E_b = V - I_a R_a$$ $$E_b = 220 - (50)(0.2)$$ $$E_b = 220 - 10$$ $$\boxed{E_b = 210\,V}$$ **Part (b) — Developed Torque:** First, convert speed to rad/s: $$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi (1000)}{60}$$ $$\omega = 104.72\,rad/s$$ Developed power (electromagnetic): $$P_{dev} = E_b \cdot I_a = 210 \times 50$$ $$P_{dev} = 10500\,W$$ Torque developed: $$T_{dev} = \dfrac{P_{dev}}{\omega} = \dfrac{10500}{104.72}$$ $$\boxed{T_{dev} \approx 100.27\,N\cdot m}$$ **Part (c) — Mechanical Power Developed:** By definition, mechanical power developed = electromagnetic power developed: $$\boxed{P_{mech} = 10.5\,kW}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Validation:** This problem mirrors the standard 14-mark KTU ESE problem format. The step-by-step approach shown here aligns with the model answer key expected by KTU examiners. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Closed-Loop Electric Drive The following Mermaid diagram represents the canonical **block-level functional architecture** of a closed-loop electric drive system. Every block corresponds to a physical or logical subsystem encountered in real industrial drives. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[REF_COMMAND_INPUT
Desired speed or torque] --> B[CONTROL_UNIT
PI or PID controller] C[SPEED_SENSOR
Tachogenerator or Encoder] --> D[FEEDBACK_SIGNAL
Actual speed measurement] D --> B B --> E[ERROR_SIGNAL
Reference minus Actual] E --> F[POWER_MODULATOR
Chopper or VFD] P[POWER_SOURCE
AC mains or DC battery] --> F F --> G[ELECTRIC_MOTOR
DC or AC type] G --> H[MECHANICAL_LOAD
Pump, conveyor, fan] G --> I[SHAFT_OUTPUT
Rotational motion] G --> C H --> J[LOAD_TORQUE_DEMAND
Variable mechanical load] J --> C ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - Arrows from `B` to `F` represent the **low-power control signal** (milliwatts). - Arrows from `P` to `F` to `G` represent the **high-power electrical flow** (kilowatts to megawatts). - Arrows from `G` to `H` represent the **mechanical power transfer** at the shaft. - The loop `G → C → D → B → E → F → G` is the **closed feedback path**. --- ### 4.2 Four-Quadrant Operation Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR Q1[QUADRANT_I
Forward Motoring
T positive, N positive] Q2[QUADRANT_II
Forward Braking
T positive, N negative] Q3[QUADRANT_III
Reverse Motoring
T negative, N negative] Q4[QUADRANT_IV
Reverse Braking
T negative, N positive] Q1 -->|Decelerate| Q2 Q2 -->|Reverse polarity| Q3 Q3 -->|Decelerate| Q4 Q4 -->|Reverse polarity| Q1 O[(ORIGIN
Zero speed zero torque)] -.-> Q1 O -.-> Q2 O -.-> Q3 O -.-> Q4 ``` **Interpretation:** - **Vertical axis** = Torque (positive = accelerating, negative = decelerating) - **Horizontal axis** = Speed (positive = forward, negative = reverse) - **Continuous transitions** between quadrants correspond to **plugging** or **regenerative braking** maneuvers. - **Origins** intersect at zero speed and zero torque — the **rest state** of the drive. --- ### 4.3 Component-Level Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SRC[POWER_SOURCE_BLOCK] A1[AC_MAINS_SUPPLY] A2[DC_BATTERY_BANK] A3[RECTIFIER_UNIT] end subgraph MOD[POWER_MODULATOR_BLOCK] B1[CHOPPER_for_DC_drive] B2[VFD_for_AC_drive] B3[CYCLOCONVERTER_for_AC_drive] end subgraph MOT[ELECTRIC_MOTOR_BLOCK] C1[DC_MOTOR] C2[INDUCTION_MOTOR] C3[BLDC_MOTOR] C4[PMSM_MOTOR] end subgraph SEN[SENSING_AND_CONTROL_BLOCK] D1[CURRENT_SENSOR] D2[SPEED_SENSOR] D3[POSITION_SENSOR] D4[CONTROLLER_PID_PLC] end A1 --> A3 A3 --> B1 A3 --> B2 A2 --> B1 A2 --> B3 B1 --> C1 B2 --> C2 B3 --> C3 B2 --> C4 C1 --> E1[MECHANICAL_LOAD] C2 --> E1 C3 --> E1 C4 --> E1 D1 --> D4 D2 --> D4 D3 --> D4 D4 --> B1 D4 --> B2 D4 --> B3 ``` **Reading the Architecture:** - The **power source block** supplies raw electrical energy. - The **power modulator block** conditions the power based on controller signals. - The **motor block** converts electrical energy into mechanical rotation. - The **sensing and control block** observes the system and commands the modulator, completing the closed loop. > [!NOTE] > This block-level representation follows the **KTU 2024 syllabus notation** for electric drives, where the system is decomposed into Source, Modulator, Motor, Load, and Sensing/Control sub-blocks. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define an electric drive and list its four essential components. **Model Answer:** An **electric drive** is a system used to control the speed, torque, and position of a mechanical load by converting and regulating electrical energy through a motor. The four essential components are: 1. **Power Source** — supplies electrical energy (mains, battery, rectifier) 2. **Power Modulator** — regulates electrical energy to the motor (chopper, VFD) 3. **Electric Motor** — converts electrical energy into mechanical energy 4. **Sensing and Control Unit** — monitors and closes the loop for accurate control A fifth optional component is the **mechanical transmission** (gearbox, coupling) between motor and load. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** What is meant by four-quadant operation of an electric drive? Name the modes of operation in each quadrant. **Model Answer:** Four-quadrant operation refers to a drive's ability to operate in all four combinations of forward/reverse rotation and motoring/braking torque on a Torque–Speed plane. | Quadrant | Mode | Torque Sign | Speed Sign | |---|---|---|---| | **Quadrant I** | Forward Motoring | Positive | Positive | | **Quadrant II** | Forward Braking (Regen/Plug) | Positive | Negative | | **Quadrant III** | Reverse Motoring | Negative | Negative | | **Quadrant IV** | Reverse Braking | Negative | Positive | This mode is essential for applications like **electric vehicles, elevators, hoists, and rolling mills** where controlled acceleration, deceleration, and reversal are routine. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working and principle of operation of a closed-loop electric drive system. List the functions of each block. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive the relationship $V/f = \text{constant}$ for an induction motor drive. Why is this relationship important in variable-frequency drives (VFDs)? **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A **(a) Closed-Loop Electric Drive Working:** The working of a closed-loop electric drive proceeds through the following sequence: 1. A **reference command** (desired speed $N_{ref}$ or torque $T_{ref}$) is issued to the control unit. 2. The **sensing unit** (tachogenerator, encoder, current sensor) measures the actual motor output. 3. The **error detector** computes $e(t) = N_{ref} - N_{actual}$. 4. The **controller** (PI or PID) processes this error and generates a corrected control signal. 5. The **power modulator** (chopper, VFD, cycloconverter) acts on the supply to vary voltage/frequency to the motor. 6. The **motor** responds by adjusting its speed/torque, and the cycle repeats continuously. **Block-wise functions:** | Block | Function | |---|---| | Source | Provides unregulated electrical power | | Power Modulator | Regulates voltage/current/frequency to motor | | Motor | Converts electrical energy to mechanical motion | | Load | Performs the desired mechanical task | | Sensors | Measure actual speed, torque, current, position | | Controller | Compares reference with feedback, generates control signal | **Block Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart TD R[REFERENCE_SPEED] --> CTRL[CONTROLLER_PI_PID] SEN[SPEED_SENSOR] --> FB[FEEDBACK_SIGNAL] FB --> CTRL CTRL --> ERR[ERROR_SIGNAL] ERR --> MOD[POWER_MODULATOR_VFD_CHOPPER] SRC[POWER_SOURCE] --> MOD MOD --> MOT[ELECTRIC_MOTOR] MOT --> LOAD[MECHANICAL_LOAD] MOT --> SEN ``` --- **(b) Derivation of V/f = Constant:** Starting from the per-phase EMF equation of an induction motor: $$E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi$$ where: - $E$ = RMS induced EMF per phase - $f$ = supply frequency (Hz) - $N$ = number of turns per phase - $\phi$ = air-gap flux per pole (Wb) Solving for flux: $$\phi = \dfrac{E}{4.44 \, f \, N}$$ For the stator voltage drop being small, $V \approx E$. Therefore: $$\phi \approx \dfrac{V}{4.44 \, f \, N}$$ For the flux to remain **constant**, the ratio $V/f$ must be held **constant**: $$\boxed{\dfrac{V}{f} = \text{constant} = k}$$ **Importance in VFDs:** 1. **Constant Torque Operation:** With constant flux, the motor delivers the same rated torque at any speed below base speed. 2. **Avoids Magnetic Saturation:** Prevents over-fluxing at low frequencies. 3. **Avoids Under-Utilization:** Prevents under-fluxing at high frequencies. 4. **Ensures Stable Drive Performance:** Critical for VFD-driven pumps, fans, and conveyors in PCRAT applications. **For speeds above base speed (field-weakening region),** the $V/f$ ratio is reduced (constant voltage mode), and torque decreases inversely with speed. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]** **(a)** Explain the four-quadrant operation of an electric drive with a Torque–Speed diagram. Discuss forward motoring, forward braking, reverse motoring, and reverse braking modes with one industrial example each. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A 220 V DC series motor has an armature resistance of $0.3\,\Omega$ and series field resistance of $0.2\,\Omega$. When running at 800 RPM, it draws 40 A. Calculate the new speed when the load torque is increased by 50% and the field flux increases by 10%. Assume the motor operates in the linear region. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B **(a) Four-Quadrant Operation:** The Torque–Speed plane is divided into four quadrants based on the sign of torque and speed: ```mermaid flowchart LR Q1[QUADRANT_I_FORWARD_MOTORING] --> Q2[QUADRANT_II_FORWARD_BRAKING_REGEN] Q2 --> Q3[QUADRANT_III_REVERSE_MOTORING] Q3 --> Q4[QUADRANT_IV_REVERSE_BRAKING] Q4 --> Q1 ``` | Quadrant | Torque | Speed | Mode | Industrial Example | |---|---|---|---|---| | **I** | Positive | Positive | Forward Motoring | Pump, conveyor belt | | **II** | Positive | Negative | Forward Braking | Downhill hoist, EV regen | | **III** | Negative | Negative | Reverse Motoring | Reversing rolling mill | | **IV** | Negative | Positive | Reverse Braking | Plug braking from forward run | **Industrial Applications:** 1. **Quadrant I — Forward Motoring:** A water pump operating in normal forward direction delivers rated torque at rated speed. 2. **Quadrant II — Forward Braking (Regenerative):** An electric vehicle descending a slope feeds kinetic energy back to the battery. 3. **Quadrant III — Reverse Motoring:** A crane lifting a load in reverse lowering mode (with controlled negative torque). 4. **Quadrant IV — Reverse Braking:** A rapid stop from forward motion by reversing torque momentarily. --- **(b) DC Series Motor Numerical Solution:** **Given Data:** $$V = 220\,V, \quad R_a = 0.3\,\Omega, \quad R_{se} = 0.2\,\Omega, \quad N_1 = 800\,RPM, \quad I_1 = 40\,A$$ **Total Series Resistance:** $$R_{total} = R_a + R_{se} = 0.3 + 0.2 = 0.5\,\Omega$$ **Step 1 — Back EMF at Initial Condition:** $$E_{b1} = V - I_1 R_{total}$$ $$E_{b1} = 220 - (40)(0.5) = 220 - 20 = 200\,V$$ **Step 2 — Initial Torque (proportional to $\phi I_a$):** For a DC series motor in the linear region, $\phi \propto I_a$: $$T_1 \propto \phi_1 I_1 = k I_1^2$$ $$T_1 \propto (40)^2 = 1600$$ **Step 3 — New Load Condition:** Load torque increases by 50%: $$T_2 = 1.5 \times T_1$$ Since $T \propto I_a^2$ for linear region: $$I_2^2 = 1.5 \times I_1^2 = 1.5 \times 1600 = 2400$$ $$I_2 = \sqrt{2400} \approx 49.0\,A$$ **Step 4 — New Flux Condition:** Flux increases by 10%: $$\phi_2 = 1.1 \phi_1$$ **Step 5 — New Back EMF:** $$E_{b2} = V - I_2 R_{total} = 220 - (49.0)(0.5)$$ $$E_{b2} = 220 - 24.5 = 195.5\,V$$ **Step 6 — Apply Speed Ratio Equation for DC Series Motor:** $$N \propto \dfrac{E_b}{\phi}$$ $$\dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{E_{b2}}{E_{b1}} \times \dfrac{\phi_1}{\phi_2}$$ $$\dfrac{N_2}{800} = \dfrac{195.5}{200} \times \dfrac{1}{1.1}$$ $$\dfrac{N_2}{800} = 0.9775 \times 0.9091$$ $$\dfrac{N_2}{800} = 0.8886$$ **Step 7 — Solve for New Speed:** $$\boxed{N_2 = 800 \times 0.8886 \approx 711\,RPM}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification Check:** Increased load torque with stronger flux causes speed to **decrease**, which is consistent with the load characteristic of a DC series motor. The result passes the physical sanity check. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > > 1. **Do not assume $\phi \propto I_a$ for all operating regions** — for series motors near saturation, this linearity breaks down. KTU examiners specifically look for the **linear region assumption** to be stated explicitly. > 2. **Avoid sign errors in four-quadrant diagrams** — Torque and Speed axes MUST be labeled with positive and negative directions explicitly. Marking them without signs loses **2 to 3 marks** instantly. > 3. **In numerical problems, ALWAYS show unit conversion** for $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$. Skipping this step loses **1 mark** in the KTU board valuation key. > 4. **For V/F derivations**, students commonly forget to mention the **assumption of small stator drop**. KTU examiners allocate a sub-mark specifically for this assumption. > 5. **In closed-loop diagrams**, ensure every block has its **function** listed separately. A diagram without functional description loses **3 marks**. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for "Working and Principle of Operation":** > > ☐ An electric drive is an **electro-mechanical energy conversion system with controlled power flow**. > > ☐ The four essential blocks are **Source → Modulator → Motor → Load**, augmented by a **Sensing/Control** unit. > > ☐ The fundamental power equation is $P_{mech} = T \cdot \omega$, where $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$. > > ☐ Closed-loop operation uses the **error signal** $e(t) = \text{Reference} - \text{Actual}$ to drive the modulator. > > ☐ **Four-quadrant operation** defines Forward Motoring, Forward Braking, Reverse Motoring, and Reverse Braking modes. > > ☐ **Regenerative braking** in Quadrant II feeds mechanical energy back to the source, improving overall efficiency. > > ☐ DC motor armature equation: $V = E_b + I_a R_a$, and speed relation $N \propto \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{\phi}$. > > ☐ DC motor torque equation: $T \propto \phi I_a$. > > ☐ Induction motor synchronous speed: $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$. > > ☐ Induction motor slip: $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$. > > ☐ **V/F control principle:** $\dfrac{V}{f} = \text{constant}$ ensures **constant air-gap flux** and **constant torque capability** below base speed. > > ☐ Power balance for any electric drive: $P_{input} = P_{Cu\,loss} + P_{rot\,loss} + P_{shaft\,output}$. > > ☐ Common power modulators include **Choppers** (DC drives), **VFDs** (AC drives), and **Cycloconverters** (high-power AC drives). > > ☐ Common control strategies include **PI, PID, Fuzzy Logic, and Field-Oriented Control (FOC)**. > > ☐ Industrial applications span **traction, robotics, HVAC, EVs, process industries, and renewable energy systems**. > > ☐ **Always state assumptions** (linear region, small stator drop, constant field) in derivations — this is a critical KTU valuation habit. > > ☐ **Always convert RPM to rad/s** before applying the torque-power formula in numerical problems.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT503 - ELECTRIC DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ STRUCTURE OF ELECTRIC DRIVE SYSTEM — KTU 2024 Module 2

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCRAT503):**
> An **Electric Drive** is a functionally integrated electromechanical system employed to control the speed, torque, position, and power flow of a mechanical load by suitably processing the electrical energy supplied from a source through power electronic converters into a form suitable for the motor.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a **manual car**. Your foot controls the **accelerator (throttle)** — this regulates how much fuel-energy goes into the engine (motor). The **gearbox** steps the power up or down (transmission). The **steering wheel** decides *direction*. The **brakes** stop motion. The **road + car body** is the *load*.

In an **Electric Drive**, electricity = fuel, the **Power Converter** = accelerator, the **Motor** = engine, the **Transmission** = mechanical coupling, the **Load** = car body, and the **Controller + Sensors** = your brain + nerves making decisions. Everything is electronic, automatic, and precise.

### 🧱 Fundamental Building Blocks (The Six Pillars)

A complete electric drive system, irrespective of whether it drives a DC or AC motor, consists of **six essential functional blocks**:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Six Pillars of Any Electric Drive:**
> 1. **Source** — Electrical energy supply (mains, battery, DC bus, renewable source).
> 2. **Power Modulator (Converter)** — Power electronics block (Rectifier / Chopper / Inverter / Cycloconverter / AC Voltage Controller) that conditions power.
> 3. **Motor** — Energy conversion device (DC, Induction, Synchronous, BLDC, PMSM, SRM).
> 4. **Sensing Unit** — Measures actual speed, current, position, voltage (tachogenerator, encoder, CT, PT, Hall sensors).
> 5. **Control Unit** — Decision-making block (analog/digital, microcontroller, DSP, FPGA, PLC).
> 6. **Load + Mechanical Transmission** — Mechanical coupling, gearbox, the driven machine.

### 📌 Standardized KTU Metrics to Memorize

- **Power Rating range:** Drives are typically classified from **fractional kW (≈ 0.1 kW)** for small appliances up to **multi-MW (≈ 10 MW)** for industrial mills, locomotives, and marine propulsion.
- **Switching frequency of modern converters:** **2 kHz – 20 kHz** (PWM range).
- **Conversion efficiency of drive stage:** typically **85 % – 96 %**.
- **Torque unit:** **N·m**; **Speed unit:** **rad/s** (SI), **rpm** (practical).

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🔍 Block-by-Block Functional Breakdown

**Block 1 — Source**
- Provides raw electrical energy: single-phase / three-phase AC mains, DC battery, photovoltaic array, or a DC link.
- **Why needed?** No drive can operate without a defined input source; the source determines the converter topology.

**Block 2 — Power Modulator (Converter)**
- Performs controlled energy transfer between source and motor.
- **AC → DC** : Diode bridge / Controlled Rectifier.
- **DC → DC** : Chopper (Buck, Boost, Buck-Boost).
- **DC → AC** : VSI (Voltage Source Inverter), CSI (Current Source Inverter).
- **AC → AC** : Cycloconverter, AC Voltage Controller.

**Block 3 — Motor**
- Converts electrical energy into mechanical rotational energy.
- Selection depends on **application** (constant torque, constant power, positioning, traction).

**Block 4 — Control Unit**
- Implements control algorithms: **V/f control**, **Vector (Field-Oriented) Control**, **Direct Torque Control (DTC)**, **PID loops**.
- Uses reference signals vs. sensed signals → computes error → fires converter gates via **PWM / SPWM / SVPWM**.

**Block 5 — Sensing Unit**
- Provides real-time feedback. Without it, the drive is **open-loop** (less accurate).

**Block 6 — Load + Transmission**
- The *purpose* of the entire drive. Includes gearbox, pulleys, belts, couplings.

### 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|-------------------|---------|-------|
